title: Spreadsheets vs. Databases
obj: Students will be able to compare spreadsheets and databases and recommend the right tool for a given data task.
content: quick exploration, ad-hoc analysis, small datasets (<100k rows), non-technical users
row limits, one editor at a time, formulas break silently, no enforced schema
concurrent access, enforced schema, relational integrity, scales to billions of rows, query language (SQL)
setup overhead, requires knowledge of SQL, less interactive than a sheet
if the data grows, is shared by multiple users, or needs consistency guarantees → database
assess: Recommend: for each of 5 scenarios (classroom roster, e-commerce orders, household budget, city census, Twitter feed), pick sheet or database
Design: sketch a table schema for a library's book-loan records
Short answer: what breaks in a spreadsheet when 10 people edit at once?

title: Introduction to SQL and DBMS
obj: Students will be able to describe what a DBMS is and write simple SQL SELECT queries with filtering and sorting.
content: manages storage, concurrency, integrity, and query execution; examples: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, SQL Server
`SELECT column_list FROM table;` — the fundamental query
filter rows by a condition: `WHERE age > 18 AND city = 'Atlanta'`
sort results and cap how many rows come back
combine rows from two tables on a matching key (covered in a later lesson)
SQL for structured relational data; NoSQL (MongoDB, Redis) for document/key-value/graph data
assess: Write 3 SQL queries against a students table: list all, filter by grade>80, order by last_name
Lab: run the queries on an SQLite file in the browser or DB Browser
Short answer: what is the role of a DBMS that a spreadsheet doesn't provide?

title: Datasets and Pandas DataFrames
obj: Students will be able to define a dataset, load one into a Pandas DataFrame, and perform basic inspection and selection.
content: a collection of related records; usually a CSV/JSON/Parquet file or a DB table
rows = records, columns = features/attributes; metadata about what columns mean
Pandas' tabular structure; labeled rows + labeled columns; behaves like a supercharged spreadsheet
`pd.read_csv(path)`, `pd.read_json`, `pd.read_sql`; first step of every DS project
`.head()`, `.info()`, `.describe()`, `.shape` — what you do before anything else
assess: Lab: load a sample CSV into Pandas; run .head, .info, .describe and summarize
Write code: filter the DataFrame to rows where 'score' > 90 and sort by name
Short answer: why do practitioners always run .info() and .describe() first?

title: Statistics for AI and Data Science
obj: Students will be able to describe key statistical concepts used in DS/AI and apply them to simple analyses.
content: fits a line/curve to data to predict a numeric outcome; R² measures fit quality
null vs. alternative hypothesis, p-value, alpha (significance level); 'p < 0.05' rule
analysis of variance — compares means across multiple groups
how a sample's statistic varies across repeated samples; basis for confidence intervals
two variables moving together doesn't mean one causes the other (classic example: ice cream and drowning)
use stats to quantify uncertainty — reporting a number without it is usually a mistake
assess: Compute (in a sheet or Python): regression line slope and R² for a small dataset
Interpret: given a p-value of 0.03 with alpha=0.05, what conclusion can you draw?
Short answer: find a headline that confuses correlation and causation and explain the error

title: Balanced vs. Imbalanced Datasets
obj: Students will be able to define balance in a dataset, identify when it's imbalanced, and describe the problems imbalance causes for AI.
content: roughly equal examples per class — makes training stable
one class dominates (e.g., fraud detection: 99% normal, 1% fraud)
the model can achieve 99% accuracy by always predicting 'normal' — and be useless
count examples per class; visualize with a bar chart; ratios > 10:1 warrant concern
oversample the minority, undersample the majority, SMOTE synthetic samples, weighted loss
when imbalanced, use precision/recall/F1, not accuracy
assess: Lab: use Pandas to check class balance of a sample dataset; plot counts
Describe: for a rare-disease classifier, which fix (over/under/SMOTE) would you try first and why?
Short answer: why does 99% accuracy mean nothing for fraud detection?

title: Train / Validation / Test Splits
obj: Students will be able to split a dataset into train/validation/test sets, explain the purpose of each, and avoid data leakage.
content: used to fit the model — typically 60-80% of data
used during training to tune hyperparameters and detect overfitting — typically 10-20%
held out until the very end; used once to estimate real-world performance — typically 10-20%
if you tune on your test set, you no longer have an honest measure of performance
preserve class balance across splits when data is imbalanced
info from test leaking into train (by preprocessing before split, by time-ordered data split randomly); destroys validity
assess: Code: use sklearn's train_test_split to make a 70/15/15 split
Identify: given 3 scenarios, spot the data leakage mistake
Short answer: why can't you just use the training accuracy as your report metric?

title: Data Cleansing, Collection, and Transformation
obj: Students will be able to identify common data-quality problems and apply Pandas operations to clean and transform a dataset.
content: detect (.isna().sum()); fix by drop, fill with mean/median, forward-fill, model-based imputation
.duplicated() and .drop_duplicates(); decide on subset of columns that define a duplicate
'NYC' vs 'New York' vs 'ny' — normalize with .str.lower(), .str.strip(), mapping dicts
detect with boxplots or z-score; investigate before removing — they might be real
numbers stored as strings, dates not parsed — cast with pd.to_numeric, pd.to_datetime
scaling/normalizing, one-hot encoding of categoricals, log transforms for skewed data
assess: Lab: given a messy CSV, apply cleaning steps and produce a report of before/after counts
Design: propose a cleaning plan for a survey dataset with 15% missing responses
Short answer: when should you drop rows with missing data vs. impute?

title: Evaluating Data Sources for Reliability
obj: Students will be able to evaluate data sources for reliability, recency, and suitability and identify good repositories for ML datasets.
content: who collected the data, with what methodology, how recent, is it updated regularly?
what can you legally do with the data — commercial, research, attribution required?
does the sample cover your use case? any demographic or geographic gaps?
Kaggle, UCI ML Repository, Hugging Face Datasets, Google Dataset Search, government open-data portals
no source citation, no license, broken download links, outdated, proprietary behind login
assess: Audit: pick a Kaggle dataset and evaluate it across the 5 criteria
Short answer: why might an otherwise-great dataset be inappropriate for commercial use?
Research: find one government open-data portal and list 3 datasets it publishes

title: Pandas for Data Analysis
obj: Students will be able to use core Pandas operations (groupby, merge, apply) to manipulate tabular data.
content: `df.groupby('category')['value'].mean()` — the SQL GROUP BY of Pandas
`pd.merge(left, right, on='key')` — combine DataFrames like SQL JOINs
run a function across rows, columns, or cells — use sparingly vs. vectorized operations
`df['c'] = df['a'] + df['b']` — faster than loops; always try this first
reshape long → wide and back; parallels Excel pivot but scriptable
assess: Lab: given a sales DataFrame, compute sum-of-revenue by region and product using groupby
Lab: merge a customers DataFrame with an orders DataFrame on customer_id
Short answer: why is a vectorized operation faster than a Python loop in Pandas?

title: Ethical Issues in Data Science
obj: Students will be able to identify ethical issues in data science work and describe mitigation strategies.
content: was the data collected with informed consent? can individuals opt out? PII handling
does the dataset represent all groups? are analyses sensitive to protected attributes?
can others verify your work? document code, data versions, random seeds
will publishing this analysis cause harm to any group? weigh harms vs. benefits
who funded the analysis? disclose stakes; data can be cherry-picked under pressure
analyses that help also can be weaponized — anticipate misuse
assess: Analyze: describe the ethics issue in a real case (Cambridge Analytica, Target-pregnancy-prediction, etc.)
Design: write 5 ethical guidelines for a student-data analytics project
Short answer: why is 'anonymized' data often not truly anonymous?
